name: Leigh R. Hochberg
role: Professor of Engineering & Neurology; BrainGate PI
bio: Leigh Hochberg leads the BrainGate clinical trial program, the longest-running series of implanted BCI studies in humans. His team has enabled people with tetraplegia and ALS to control robotic arms, computer cursors, and synthesizers using intracortical neural signals recorded from motor cortex.
notableWork: BrainGate2 clinical trial (2006-present)
First demonstration of BrainGate cursor control (Nature 2006)
Highest-performance intracortical speech BCI (Nature 2021, with Shenoy/Willett)

name: Krishna V. Shenoy
role: Professor of Electrical Engineering, Neurobiology, Neurosurgery; Howard Hughes Medical Institute Investigator
bio: Krishna Shenoy was one of the foremost figures in systems neuroscience and BMI research. His lab developed foundational insights into motor cortex neural dynamics, preparatory activity, rotational dynamics, and translated them into high-performance BCIs including the handwriting decoder achieving 90 characters per minute.
notableWork: Rotational dynamics in motor cortex (Nature 2012)
Handwriting BCI, 90 char/min (Nature 2021)
Co-founder of BrainGate consortium

name: Miguel A. L. Nicolelis
role: Professor of Neurobiology; Co-director, Duke Center for Neuroengineering
bio: A pioneer of brain-machine interfaces, Nicolelis demonstrated the first real-time control of a robotic arm from multi-electrode cortical recordings in non-human primates. He later directed the Walk Again Project, which enabled a paralyzed patient to kick a soccer ball at the 2014 FIFA World Cup using an EEG-controlled exoskeleton.
notableWork: First real-time robotic arm control from cortex in primates (Nature 1999)
Walk Again Project exoskeleton (2014 FIFA World Cup)
Brain-to-brain cortical coupling across two rats (Scientific Reports 2013)

name: John P. Donoghue
affiliation: Brown University / École Polytechnique Fédérale de Lausanne
bio: John Donoghue founded the BrainGate program in 2001, creating the first FDA-approved intracortical BCI research device. His work on motor cortex population coding and neural decoding algorithms laid the foundation for modern clinical BCIs. He later directed the Wyss Center for Bio and Neuroengineering in Geneva.
notableWork: BrainGate, first human implanted BCI (2004)
Instant neural decoding of intended movement (Nature Neuroscience 2002)
Co-inventor of the Utah Array clinical implant

name: Edward F. Chang
role: Professor of Neurological Surgery; Vice Chair of Research
bio: Edward Chang leads a lab at UCSF studying the neural basis of speech and developing ECoG-based speech BCIs. His group pioneered direct speech synthesis from cortical activity and demonstrated that entire sentences could be decoded from sensorimotor cortex signals, providing a pathway to speech restoration for locked-in patients.
notableWork: Speech synthesis from ECoG (Nature 2019)
Sentence decoding from sensorimotor cortex (NEJM 2021)
First real-time voice-to-text BCI in humans (Nature 2023)

name: Jennifer L. Collinger
affiliation: University of Pittsburgh / VA Pittsburgh Healthcare System
role: Associate Professor of Physical Medicine & Rehabilitation
bio: Jennifer Collinger conducts clinical BCI research focused on upper-limb neuroprosthetics. Her team achieved 7-dimensional robotic arm control using intracortical recordings, and later demonstrated bidirectional BCIs combining motor output with somatosensory feedback to restore touch sensation to the hand.
notableWork: 7-DOF robotic arm control from motor cortex (Lancet 2013)
Bidirectional BCI with tactile feedback (Science Translational Medicine 2016)
BrainGate2 clinical trial co-investigator

name: Sliman J. Bensmaia
role: Professor of Organismal Biology and Anatomy
bio: Sliman Bensmaia was the leading expert on the neural basis of somatosensation and how to restore tactile feedback to BCI users. His lab decoded the neural codes of touch in primary somatosensory cortex and designed microstimulation patterns that elicit naturalistic tactile percepts without causing adaptation or discomfort.
notableWork: Tactile feedback restores robotic arm control (Science Translational Medicine 2014)
Neural code of somatosensory cortex for texture (PNAS 2009)
Closed-loop somatosensory stimulation (Science 2016)

name: Andrew B. Schwartz
role: Professor of Neurobiology; Director, Motor Control Lab
bio: Andrew Schwartz is known for fundamental discoveries in motor cortex population coding and pioneering demonstrations of dexterous neuroprosthetic limb control. His lab recorded the first coordinated multi-joint arm movement controlled by cortical signals in primates, and later translated this to human participants achieving 7-DOF control.
notableWork: Population vector coding of movement direction (Science 1988)
Cortically controlled prosthetic arm (Neuron 2008)
3D reach-and-grasp by primate BCI (Nature 2004)

name: Thomas Oxley
role: CEO, Synchron; Adjunct Professor of Medicine
bio: Thomas Oxley is the neurovascular surgeon and neuroscientist who invented and commercializes the Stentrode, an endovascular BCI that accesses motor cortex through blood vessels, avoiding craniotomy. He completed the first-in-human Stentrode implant in Australia (2019) and subsequently launched the SWITCH trial in the US (2021).
notableWork: Stentrode concept and first human implant (Nature Biotechnology 2016)
SWITCH trial, first US endovascular BCI (2021)
Human endovascular neural recording and stimulation (Lancet 2022)

name: Niels Birbaumer
affiliation: University of Tübingen / Wyss Center Geneva
role: Professor of Medical Psychology and Behavioral Neurobiology
bio: Niels Birbaumer is a pioneer of non-invasive BCI for completely locked-in patients. His lab developed slow cortical potential (SCP) neurofeedback as a communication channel for ALS patients and later extended this to fNIRS and invasive approaches for individuals in whom even eye movement is lost.
notableWork: First BCI for completely locked-in ALS patients (Nature 1999)
fNIRS BCI for completely locked-in syndrome (PLOS Biology 2017)
Brain-computer communication without external signals (Journal of Neural Engineering 2014)

name: Rafael Yuste
role: Professor of Biological Sciences and Neuroscience; NeuroRights Foundation Director
bio: Rafael Yuste is a neuroscientist who pioneered two-photon calcium imaging of neural circuits and co-founded the US BRAIN Initiative. Beyond his lab work, he is the leading advocate for neurorights, legal protections for mental privacy in the era of BCIs, and helped write the world's first neurorights legislation (Chile, 2021).
notableWork: Two-photon calcium imaging of cortical circuits (1992-)
Co-founder and early co-author of the BRAIN Initiative (2013)
Neurorights framework and Chilean neurorights law (2021)

name: Vikash Gilja
role: Associate Professor of Electrical and Computer Engineering
bio: Vikash Gilja develops high-performance real-time neural decoding algorithms for BCI systems. He created the ReFIT-Kalman filter, which substantially improved cursor control performance in the BrainGate trial, and now works on expanding BCI access through low-power, implantable electronics and improved decoder generalization.
notableWork: ReFIT Kalman decoder (Nature Neuroscience 2012)
High-performance BCI clinical demonstration (Nature Medicine 2015)
Long-term BCI decoder stability (Neuron 2018)
